context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m trying to do the Spider Diagram for my case study city, but I’m not totally sure how to organize the info around the 5 pathways. Could you help me figure out what kind of evidence or examples I should be looking for in each pathway?
exchanges.tutor: Good question to start with! Before we dig in — what do you already know about the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation from the course? Can you name them or describe any of them in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a broad orientation question about how to approach the Spider Diagram. Rather than explaining the 5 pathways for them, I should prompt them to reflect on what they already know from the course material. This keeps the discovery process student-led and avoids front-loading information they should be retrieving themselves. I'll ask what they already know about the 5 pathways to assess their starting point.

exchanges.student: I only remember them kind of vaguely. I think one is about governance/policy stuff, and maybe another is about technology or infrastructure, but I’m mixing them up. Could you give me a quick refresher on the 5 pathways so I can sort them out?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track with a couple of those! But rather than me listing them out, I'd encourage you to go back to your course materials — lecture notes, readings, or slides from this week. The pathways are the backbone of this assignment, so it's worth refreshing directly from the source. Once you have them in front of you, come back and we can work through how to apply them to your city!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the 5 pathways directly, which would bypass their own learning process. Instead, I should redirect them to their course materials, since this is foundational content they should retrieve themselves. I'll acknowledge what they partially remember and encourage them to go back to the source rather than giving them the list.

exchanges.student: Okay, fair. I’ll check the course page and pull the exact pathway names, then come back with them so I don’t keep guessing.
exchanges.tutor: Perfect — that's exactly the right move. See you when you're back!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is taking a productive step by going back to the course materials. This is exactly the right behavior to encourage. A brief, positive acknowledgment is appropriate here — no need to add more scaffolding at this stage.

exchanges.student: Thanks — I’ll be back once I’ve got the list.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stepping away to retrieve their course materials. Nothing more needs to be said here — a brief, encouraging acknowledgment is sufficient.

exchanges.student: Okay, I found them. The 5 pathways are planning and design, innovative technologies, governance and institutions, social behavior, and finance. I’m still not sure how to turn those into spider diagram notes for my city though.
exchanges.tutor: Great, you've got them! Now, what's your case study city? And let's start small — pick one pathway that feels most intuitive to you, and tell me what comes to mind when you think about how your city might be acting on it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the 5 pathways, which is good progress. Now I should help them move toward applying these to their city without doing the work for them. A good Socratic move here is to ask them to pick one pathway and think about what kind of evidence or action in their city might relate to it. This keeps the discovery process going and is bite-sized.

exchanges.student: My city is Boston. The easiest one for me is probably planning and design, because I know Boston has climate resilience stuff, but I’m not sure what counts. Would things like coastal flood plans, zoning changes, or green infrastructure go under that?
exchanges.tutor: Those are promising examples! But before I weigh in — what do you think connects those three things? What makes them feel like 'planning and design' rather than, say, 'governance and institutions'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good intuitive connections between concrete examples and the planning and design pathway. Rather than confirming or denying their examples, I should ask them to reason through why those examples fit — this deepens understanding and prepares them to apply the same logic to the other pathways independently.

exchanges.student: I think planning and design is more about the physical layout of the city and how places get shaped, while governance and institutions is more about who makes the decisions and what rules or agencies are involved. So coastal flood plans, zoning, and green infrastructure seem more like planning/design because they change land use or the built environment. Is that basically the right distinction?
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid distinction — good reasoning. One thing worth sitting with: could zoning changes also involve governance and institutions? Think about who sets zoning rules and how. Does that change where you'd put it, or does it belong in both? Then let's move on to another pathway for Boston!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely strong conceptual distinction between planning/design and governance/institutions. This deserves positive acknowledgment. However, I should nudge them to notice that some examples (like zoning changes) could potentially straddle both categories, which will sharpen their thinking. I won't give the answer but will prompt reflection. I also want to keep momentum going toward filling out the diagram.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think zoning could definitely touch both. The rule itself comes from governance, but the effect shows up in planning/design, so maybe I can mention it in both if I explain the angle. For another pathway, I’m guessing innovative technologies could be stuff like building energy systems, EV charging, or flood monitoring tech in Boston?
exchanges.tutor: Good handle on the zoning overlap. On innovative technologies — those examples make sense conceptually, but do you actually know if Boston has specific initiatives around any of those? The spider diagram should be grounded in what your city is actually doing, not just what seems plausible. What have you found, or where might you look?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student handled the zoning overlap well and is now making reasonable intuitive connections for innovative technologies. I should briefly affirm their reasoning on zoning, then push them slightly deeper on the technologies examples — specifically, can they connect those examples to Boston's actual climate action efforts rather than just general guesses? This moves them toward evidence-based research, which is what the assignment requires.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that’s where I get stuck — I can think of plausible examples, but I don’t want to make them up. I probably need to look at Boston’s climate plan or city sustainability pages for named programs, right? If I find a specific program, I can sort it into the pathway instead of guessing.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — primary sources like Boston's official climate plan are the way to go. That's solid research instinct. Go find a specific program or initiative, and when you come back, tell me what you found and which pathway you'd put it under. We still have three pathways to work through!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at exactly the right research approach on their own — go to primary sources like Boston's climate plan. This is a great moment to affirm and push them toward action. I should encourage them to do that research and come back with findings, keeping momentum going. No need for extra scaffolding here.

exchanges.student: Got it — I’ll look for actual Boston programs and then try sorting them by pathway when I come back. I think that’ll help me stop being so vague.
exchanges.tutor: That's the right approach — specific evidence is what will make your spider diagram strong. See you when you're back with some findings!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a clear plan and good momentum. A brief encouraging sign-off is all that's needed here. No additional scaffolding is necessary.
